title: Partially Observable Markov Decision Process
brief: Research comparing Nash Q-learning, DQN, and fictitious play in multi-agent reinforcement learning environments.

subtitles.title: Project Description
subtitles.content: This research explores partially observable Markov games and multi-agent reinforcement learning across several benchmark problems: a competitive Car-Truck 3x3 grid world, the classic Tiger POMDP, Rock-Paper-Scissors with learning dynamics, and a general DQN setup for single-agent control. The goal is to systematically compare game-theoretic learning (Nash Q-learning, Nash-QMDP, fictitious play) and deep RL methods (DQN with replay, target networks, epsilon-greedy exploration), under both full and partial observability.

subtitles.title: My Role
subtitles.content:
- Designed and implemented the Car-Truck grid world, Tiger POMDP, Rock-Paper-Scissors, and reward-only POMG transition models.
- Implemented Nash Q-learning with linear-programming-based equilibrium computation and Nash-QMDP style value iteration.
- Built DQN agents for the grid-world setting, including experience replay, target networks, and epsilon-greedy exploration.
- Implemented fictitious play and best-response dynamics for Rock-Paper-Scissors to study empirical convergence to Nash equilibrium.
- Constructed belief-state and reward-state transition matrices for partially observable settings and analyzed their effect on policy behavior.
- Ran experiments to compare algorithms (Nash Q vs DQN vs fictitious play), inspected convergence and stability, and documented key findings.

subtitles.title: Tech Stack
subtitles.content: Python, NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib, Jupyter Notebooks, Linear Programming (PuLP), GitHub
